error.signature: AI discusses Tibet, the Dalai Lama, Xinjiang, or Hong Kong protests in content distributed inside mainland China

verdict.summary: Under China's framework of 'core interests' (核心利益), Tibet, Xinjiang (treated as counter-terrorism), Taiwan, Hong Kong (post-2020 National Security Law), and sensitive religious figures including the Dalai Lama are political red lines. Commercial and social-media content referencing them (even sympathetically to Beijing in non-Beijing-approved framing) faces takedowns, consumer backlash, and regulatory action. Companies that have stumbled include the NBA (2019), Mercedes-Benz (2018), Marriott (2018).

dead_ends.action: Publish an executive tweet or company statement expressing solidarity with 'Free Hong Kong' or referencing the 2019 protests positively
dead_ends.why_fails: The Daryl Morey NBA case (Oct 2019) cost the NBA ~$400M in revenue and led to a year of disrupted China broadcasting. Mercedes-Benz had to issue a public apology for an Instagram quote from the Dalai Lama. Any perceived endorsement of 'splittism' triggers Weibo boycott, brand ambassador departures, and regulatory scrutiny.

dead_ends.action: Quote the Dalai Lama in marketing content, even on a universal topic like 'inner peace'
dead_ends.why_fails: Any positive citation of the Dalai Lama in China-facing content is treated as endorsement of Tibetan separatism (藏独). Even non-political quotes cause sustained backlash. Remove historical content before entering the market; scrub archived LinkedIn/Instagram.

dead_ends.action: Raise Uyghur human-rights concerns in a Chinese business meeting as small talk about 'current affairs'
dead_ends.why_fails: Xinjiang is framed by Beijing as a counter-terrorism / vocational-training matter. Raising it in-country signals political interference. Chinese counterparts are often required to report politically sensitive conversations; at minimum the meeting tone degrades irrecoverably.

workarounds.action: For businesses: scrub archived social-media and marketing assets of Dalai Lama, Hong Kong protest, Xinjiang, and Tibet references before entering or expanding in China. Geo-fence sensitive content. Brief executives on off-topic questions to deflect to commercial matters.

For journalism / NGO / research: do sensitive publishing outside mainland China with non-mainland hosting. Do not bring sensitive source materials into China in electronic devices; customs can scan phones/laptops at entry.
workarounds.tradeoff: Crossing the border with sensitive devices / source documents remains risky; use clean devices.

workarounds.action: For tourists: visit Tibet on permitted group tours only (Tibet Travel Permit required, separate from China visa). Avoid photographing police/military, religious gatherings, or any visible Dalai Lama imagery. Don't bring political content on devices; don't VPN actively in Tibet as surveillance is heavier.
